name: Vidyaranya
dates_notes: Vijayanagara court records; dual identity as Mādhavācārya (administrator) and Vidyāraṇya (sannyāsin) attested.

dates_evidence.description: Vijayanagara royal records attest his administrative role and sannyāsa-name.
Manuscript colophons of the Pañcadaśī, Sarva-Darśana-Saṃgraha, and other works confirm dual identity.

lineage_polemical.note: Vidyāraṇya's Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha (under the Mādhavācārya identity) surveys the Pratyabhijñā-darśana of Utpaladeva and Abhinavagupta as one of the principal non-Vedāntic darśanas, treating it with disciplined fairness rather than as a cursory rival.
The Pratyabhijñā chapter of the Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha takes Abhinavagupta's articulation of svātantrya, ābhāsa-vāda, and pratyabhijñā as its primary source for the school.

core_thesis: Vidyāraṇya stands at the institutional and philosophical apex of fourteenth-century Advaita. As Mādhavācārya he served the early Vijayanagara court and, with his brother Sāyaṇa, presided over a comprehensive scholastic project (so the Vijayanagara court records and the colophons of the *Sarva-Darśana-Saṃgraha* and Sāyaṇa's Vedic *bhāṣyas*; cf. Sastri, *Vidyāraṇya: A Study*, 1972); as Vidyāraṇya he became Śaṅkarācārya of Sṛṅgeri (so the *Sṛṅgeri Guru-Paramparā* records). Doctrinally he is firmly within the Vivaraṇa line: Brahman is the locus of *bhāva-rūpā-anirvacanīya-avidyā* (*Pañcadaśī* I.15–16, *māyā* and *avidyā* as the two faces of Brahman's *prakṛti*; cf. Prakāśātman, *Pañca-Pādikā-Vivaraṇa* on *Brahma-Sūtra* 1.1.1), and the *jīva* is *cidābhāsa* (*Pañcadaśī* VI.1–10, the *citra-dīpa* chapter). His distinctive synthetic contribution is twofold. First, he treats the two competing technical analyses of the *jīva* — *pratibimba-vāda* (Sarvajñātman, *Saṃkṣepa-Śārīraka* 1.319; Prakāśātman, *Pañca-Pādikā-Vivaraṇa* on 1.1.1: *jīva* as reflection of Brahman in *avidyā*) and *avaccheda-vāda* (Vāchaspati's *Bhāmatī* on *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 1.1.1, the *jīva* as Brahman delimited by *antaḥkaraṇa*) — as genuine *prakriyā*-level alternatives within a single Vivaraṇa framework (*Pañcadaśī* VI.6–10 and VIII.5–7), rather than positions one of which must be wrong. Second, he gives a systematic exposition of *jīvanmukti* as a precise technical state, not a metaphor (*Jīvanmukti-Viveka*, *prathama-prakaraṇa*): *avidyā* has been removed, *prārabdha-karma* sustains the body until exhausted (*Jīvanmukti-Viveka* I.5; cf. Śaṅkara, *Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya* 4.1.15), and *vāsanā-kṣaya* and *manonāśa* are required for the cessation of residual mental activity (*Jīvanmukti-Viveka* III.1–10, drawing on *Yoga-Vāsiṣṭha* 6.1.61–69). He combines high *advaita-siddhānta* with the practice-oriented yogic idiom of the *Yoga-Vāsiṣṭha* (the integration is programmatic in *Jīvanmukti-Viveka* III–IV); the *Pañcadaśī* derives its enduring pedagogical role from this combination (Hiriyanna, *Outlines*, 1932, ch. on Vivaraṇa-Advaita).

engaged_works.title: Panchadashi
engaged_works.summary: Independent didactic *prakaraṇa* in fifteen chapters, divided into three pentads (*pañcakas*): *viveka-pañcaka* (the *tattva-*, *mahāvākya-*, *pañcakośa-*, *dvaita-*, and *mahāvākyārtha-viveka* chapters of analysis); *dīpa-pañcaka* (the *citra-*, *tṛpti-*, *kūṭastha-*, *dhyāna-*, and *nāṭaka-dīpa* chapters of illumination); and *ānanda-pañcaka* (bliss). Vidyāraṇya works through the *avasthā-traya* and *pañca-kośa-viveka* of the Upaniṣads, the analysis of the Mahāvākya, the integration of *avaccheda-* and *pratibimba-vāda* on the *jīva*, the cosmological and psychological roles of *māyā* and avidyā, the metaphysics of *jīvanmukti*, and the experiential character of *brahmānanda*. The verse is clear *anuṣṭubh*; the illustrations are drawn from theatre, painting, and dream. The work has become the principal pedagogical *prakaraṇa* of post-Śaṅkara Advaita and is read as a primer alongside Śaṅkara's *Upadeśa-Sāhasrī* in modern *Vedānta* curricula. It is foundational for the Advaita Vedānta articulated to general audiences from the eighteenth century onward.

engaged_works.title: Jivanmukti-Viveka
engaged_works.summary: Independent *prakaraṇa* in five chapters devoted to the systematic exposition of *jīvanmukti*, liberation in this very life. Vidyāraṇya treats it as a precise technical state, not a metaphor. Avidyā has been removed by *brahma-jñāna*; *prārabdha-karma* sustains the body until exhausted; *vāsanā-kṣaya* and *manonāśa*, drawn programmatically from the *Yoga-Vāsiṣṭha*, are required for the cessation of residual mental activity. He distinguishes *vidvat-sannyāsa* (renunciation following the rise of knowledge) from *vividiṣā-sannyāsa* (renunciation as a means to its rise) and defends the legitimacy of *jīvanmukti* against opponents who would defer liberation to *videhamukti*. Yoga, *Yoga-Vāsiṣṭha*, and Pātañjala technical vocabulary are integrated into the Advaita framework. *Jīvanmukti* is treated as the proximate goal of spiritual practice. The work is the principal Advaita reference on the topic and the source for much of the modern Advaita understanding of the relation between knowledge, action, and continuing embodiment.

engaged_works.title: Vivarana-Prameya-Samgraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored compendium of Prakāśātman's Vivaraṇa.
engaged_works.summary: Topical compendium (*saṃgraha*) of the principal *prameyas* of Prakāśātman's *Pañcapādikā-Vivaraṇa*, organised as a systematic *prameya*-by-*prameya* presentation rather than a running commentary. Vidyāraṇya extracts and re-presents the Vivaraṇa positions on Brahman as locus of avidyā, the *bhāva-rūpā-anirvacanīya-avidyā* doctrine, the *bimba-pratibimba* analysis of the *jīva*, the rejection of *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, and the function of the Mahāvākya. The work matters as the canonical hand-list of Vivaraṇa tenets in compact form. It makes Prakāśātman's massive *Vivaraṇa* navigable for later students and is one of the standard references through which the Vivaraṇa positions enter post-fourteenth-century Advaita pedagogy and the *Advaita-Siddhi*-era polemics.

engaged_works.title: Sarva-Darshana-Samgraha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed to Mādhava (= Vidyāraṇya); modern scholarship accepts the ascription though some debate the exact compositional role.
engaged_works.summary: Doxographical *saṃgraha* presenting sixteen *darśanas* in ascending order of claimed approximation to truth, beginning with the Cārvāka and culminating in Śaṅkara's Advaita. Each chapter expounds a school in its own technical idiom: Bauddha, Jaina, Rāmānujīya, Pūrṇaprajña (Madhva), Nakulīśa-Pāśupata, Śaiva, Pratyabhijñā, Raseśvara, Aulūkya (Vaiśeṣika), Akṣapāda (Nyāya), Jaimini, Pāṇinīya, Sāṃkhya, Pātañjala, and finally Śāṅkara. The work is universally cited as a principal Sanskrit source for opposed-school positions. Institutionally it is the single most influential pre-modern Indian doxography. Methodologically it is a paradigm of *pūrvapakṣa*-fidelity: schools are presented in their own voice with their canonical citations before being relativised to the Advaita climax. Some modern scholars debate the exact compositional role of Mādhava as against his collaborators; the work is firmly within the Vidyāraṇya–Sāyaṇa Vijayanagara scholarly project.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
cidānandamayabrahmapratibimbasamanvitā |
tamo-rajaḥ-sattvaguṇā prakṛtir dvividhā ca sā || 15 ||
sattvaśuddhyaviśuddhibhyāṃ māyāvidye ca te mate |
māyābimbo vaśīkṛtya tāṃ syāt sarvajña īśvaraḥ || 16 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: endowed with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss
having the guṇas tamas, rajas, and sattva
through the purity and impurity of sattva

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: tamaś ca rajaś ca sattvaṃ ca guṇā yasmin sā

verb_modality.note: Predicative optative used here in a definitional sense.

key_passages.english_close: Prakṛti is endowed with the reflection of Brahman consisting of consciousness and bliss, and is possessed of the guṇas tamas, rajas, and sattva. And that prakṛti is twofold. Through the purity and the impurity of sattva, those two [parts of prakṛti] are regarded as māyā and avidyā [respectively]. The reflection in māyā, having brought that [māyā] under its control, becomes the omniscient Īśvara.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: primordial materiality or undifferentiated causal matrix
the pure-sattva causal power associated with Īśvara
ignorance, here the impure-sattva form of causal unknowing
the Lord, omniscient Brahman as causally related to the world

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a position-establishing locus, not a detachable slogan: it gives Vidyāraṇya's compact Vivaraṇa mapping of twofold prakṛti into māyā and avidyā and explicitly links lordship to the māyā-reflection. Because the Sanskrit corpus on disk is scan-only, I prioritized passages whose wording could be securely cross-checked against the local English translation; this one is still foundational rather than opportunistic.
key_passages.source_edition: Pañcadaśī with Modaka ṭīkā, Sanskrit edition, local archive PDF: materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vidyaranya_panchadasi_sanskrit_modaka_tika_archive.pdf

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
yathā muñjād iveṣīkām ātmā yuktyā samuddhṛtaḥ |
śarīratritayād evaṃ paraṃ brahmaiva jāyate || 42 ||

pada_analysis.morphology: particle + acc sg fem (sandhi form: iva + iṣīkām)

pada_analysis.gloss: comes to be, is recognized as

verb_modality.note: Used in the sense of recognition or realization, not literal production.

key_passages.english_close: Just as the inner fibre is drawn out from muñja grass. So too, when the self is extracted by reasoning from the triad of bodies, it [is recognized to] become nothing but the supreme Brahman.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: the reed or grass from which the inner pith is carefully drawn out
the three bodies: gross, subtle, and causal

key_passages.why_this_passage: This verse is the payoff of the chapter's discriminative method: it states, in Vidyāraṇya's own pedagogical idiom, how the self is analytically separated from the layered body-complex and recognized as Brahman. It fairly represents his teaching because the Pañcadaśī is meant to train exactly this style of viveka rather than merely assert identity abstractly.
key_passages.source_edition: Pañcadaśī with Modaka ṭīkā, Sanskrit edition, local archive PDF: materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vidyaranya_panchadasi_sanskrit_modaka_tika_archive.pdf

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
tābhyāṃ nirvicikitsārthe cetasaḥ sthāpitasya yat |
ekatānattvam etad dhi nididhyāsanam ucyate || 54 ||
dhyātṛdhyāne parityajya kramād dhyeyaikagocaram |
nivātadīpavac cittaṃ samādhir abhidhīyate || 55 ||

pada_analysis.gloss: in the matter free of doubt
having the object of meditation alone as its range
like a lamp in a windless place

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: dhyeyaṃ eva eko gocaro yasya tat

key_passages.english_close: When, by those two [śravaṇa and manana], the mind has been established in the matter freed from doubt. The one-pointed continuity that ensues — this indeed is called nididhyāsana. Having left aside meditator and meditation, [and] step by step having only the object of meditation as its range. The mind, then, like a lamp in a windless place, is designated samādhi.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: deep contemplative assimilation of the teaching already heard and reasoned through

key_passages.why_this_passage: Vidyāraṇya is not content with a bare claim that knowledge liberates; he specifies the internal sequence by which śravaṇa and manana mature into nididhyāsana and samādhi. That makes this a representative passage for his synthesis of Advaita pedagogy with contemplative discipline, rather than an isolated yogic flourish.
key_passages.source_edition: Pañcadaśī with Modaka ṭīkā, Sanskrit edition, local archive PDF: materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/vidyaranya_panchadasi_sanskrit_modaka_tika_archive.pdf
